target.name: Lappaceolides A and B
target.motivation: To verify the Ragasa biosynthetic proposal of lappaceolides arising from a Michael/oxa-Michael dimerization of siphonodin by developing a biomimetic total synthesis of racemic lappaceolides A and B.

success_criteria.free_text: Achieve a biomimetic total synthesis of racemic lappaceolides A and B directly from siphonodin in a single step, confirming the biosynthetic hypothesis.

key_disconnections.reasoning: Based on the biosynthetic hypothesis, this disconnection leads to a dimeric diol intermediate.

key_disconnections.bond_description: dimeric diol to two siphonodin molecules
key_disconnections.reasoning: This disconnection directly leads to the proposed monomer, siphonodin.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy is based on Ragasa's siphonodin homodimerization hypothesis, envisioning a combined vinylogous-Michael-oxa-Michael domino reaction to assemble lappaceolides A and B directly from siphonodin in a single step.

thought.background: Lappaceolides A and B are tricyclic monoterpene lactones with three contiguous stereogenic centers. They were co-isolated with siphonodin, and a biosynthetic hypothesis suggests they arise from a Michael/oxa-Michael dimerization of siphonodin.
thought.gap: The biosynthetic hypothesis needs experimental verification through a biomimetic total synthesis.
thought.decision: To verify the biosynthetic proposal, a biomimetic total synthesis of racemic lappaceolides A and B will be pursued, focusing on a vinylogous-Michael-oxa-Michael domino reaction from siphonodin.

outcome.observation: Retrosynthetic analysis identified siphonodin as the key precursor, suggesting a vinylogous-Michael-oxa-Michael domino reaction.

evidence.text_span: Devising a total synthesis approach around Ragasa’s siphonodin (3) homodimerization hypothesis, the C3−O bond of lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) can be disconnected in an oxa-Michael sense, yielding dimeric diol 4 (Scheme 1B). This dimeric compound 4 can be further disconnected in a vinylogous Michael addition, yielding two molecules of siphonodin (3). With these disconnections in hand, we envisioned that they could be combined in the forward sense into a vinylogous-Michael−oxa-Michael domino reaction to assemble both lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) directly from siphonodin (3) in a single step.

thought.background: Siphonodin (3) is the proposed monomer for the biomimetic dimerization. A known Wittig-olefination-lactonization domino reaction can be used to synthesize it from dihydroxyacetone (7) and ylide (8).
thought.gap: Siphonodin (3) needs to be synthesized in sufficient quantities for the dimerization studies.
thought.decision: To obtain siphonodin (3), dihydroxyacetone (7) will be treated with ylide (8) in DCM at room temperature for 24 hours, expecting a Wittig-olefination-lactonization domino reaction.

outcome.observation: Siphonodin (3) was successfully synthesized in 78% yield, allowing for multigram production.

evidence.text_span: Based on the above analysis, the campaign was initiated by synthesis of the postulated biosynthetic monomer siphonodin (3). This was achieved by treating dihydroxyacetone (7) with the ylide 8, resulting in a Wittig-olefination−lactonization domino reaction to deliver the siphonodin (3) in 78% yield (Scheme 2).6a,b The approach was readily scalable, allowing production of multigram quantities of siphonodin (3).

thought.background: Siphonodin (3) has been successfully synthesized. The next step is to attempt the biomimetic vinylogous-Michael-oxa-Michael homodimerization.
thought.gap: Finding suitable conditions for the dimerization of siphonodin (3) to lappaceolides A and B.
thought.decision: To achieve dimerization, siphonodin (3) will be subjected to various conditions, including different temperatures, solvents (THF, water, acetonitrile, DCM, PhMe), and a range of bases (K2CO3, Na2CO3, Cs2CO3, TBAF, Et3N, DBU, NaH, DABCO, NaHMDS, KOtBu) and acidic additives (Zn(OTf)2, Amberlyst 15, and p-TsOH).

outcome.observation: Numerous attempts at dimerizing 3 were unsuccessful, with no detectable conversion to lappaceolides by 1H NMR.

evidence.text_span: We then turned our attention to the biomimetic vinylogous-Michael−oxa-Michael homodimerization of 3. Despite the mechanistically promising biomimetic domino reaction, numerous attempts at dimerizing 3 were unsuccessful. Dimerizing 3 at room temperature or elevated temperatures in a range of solvents (THF, water, acetonitrile, DCM, PhMe) with a range of bases K2CO3, Na2CO3, Cs2CO3, TBAF, Et3N, DBU, NaH, DABCO, NaHMDS, KOtBu) and acidic additives (Zn(OTf)2, Amberlyst 15, and p-TsOH) gave no detectable (1H NMR) conversion of 3 to lappaceolides (see the SI).

thought.background: Initial attempts at dimerization were unsuccessful. The Lawrence group faced a similar problem and found success with specific conditions for a Michael-oxa-Michael sequence.
thought.gap: Identify conditions that enable the biomimetic dimerization of siphonodin (3).
thought.decision: To overcome the lack of reactivity, the Lawrence conditions (20 mol% K2CO3 in 1,2-DCE at 70 °C) will be applied to the dimerization of siphonodin (3).

outcome.observation: Applying Lawrence conditions resulted in 5% conversion to lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) with a dr = 54:46 after 12 hours, indicating initial success.

evidence.text_span: A related problem was faced by the Lawrence group in their total synthesis of (−)-angiopterlactone B while developing a domino reaction housing a Michael−oxa-Michael sequence.7 Implementing the Lawrence conditions, using 20 mol % of K2CO3 in 1,2-DCE at 70 °C, in our system, we found a 5% conversion to lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) in dr = 54:46 after 12 h.

thought.background: Initial success with Lawrence conditions showed 5% conversion. Further optimization is needed to improve yield and conversion.
thought.gap: Optimize the reaction conditions for the biomimetic vinylogous-Michael-oxa-Michael homodimerization of siphonodin (3) to achieve higher conversion and yield.
thought.decision: To optimize the dimerization, 10.0 equiv of Cs2CO3 will be used in 1,2-DCE at 85 °C for 4 hours, aiming for improved conversion and yield.

outcome.observation: Optimized conditions resulted in 100% conversion to lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) in a 3:2 ratio and 30% isolated yield. However, yields, conversions, and diastereomeric ratios varied from batch to batch (8-30%, 75-100%, dr 3:2-2:3). Reaction times longer than 4 hours led to lower yields, indicating product instability.

evidence.text_span: Taking this initial discovery through extensive further optimization (see the SI), we found that using 10.0 equiv of Cs2CO3 in 1,2-DCE at 85 °C in 4 h results, in the best case, in 100% conversion to lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) in a 3:2 ratio and 30% isolated yield. The yields, conversions, and diastereomeric ratios of 1 and 2 were however found to vary (8−30%, 75−100%, dr 3:2−2:3) from batch to batch. Reaction times longer than 4 h were found to result in lower yields, indicating that 1 and 2 are unstable at elevated temperatures under the reaction conditions. Careful kinetic control was, therefore, required for a successful dimerization of 3.

thought.background: Lappaceolides A and B have been synthesized, but purification from unreacted siphonodin (3) is challenging with typical flash column chromatography eluents.
thought.gap: Develop an effective purification method for lappaceolides A and B from siphonodin (3).
thought.decision: To purify the products, flash column chromatography using Et2O-MeCN-DCM (1:1:3 v/v/v) will be employed.

outcome.observation: Flash column chromatography with Et2O-MeCN-DCM (1:1:3 v/v/v) successfully separated siphonodin (3) from lappaceolides (1 and 2). Spectroscopic data matched reported values, and a single-crystal X-ray diffraction structure was obtained for cocrystallized 1 and 2.

evidence.text_span: Purification of lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) proved to be equally challenging. Using typical flash column chromatography eluent systems such as EtOAc/hexane or DCM/MeOH did not allow unreacted siphonodin (3) to be separated from 1 and 2. Ultimately, flash column chromatography using Et2O−MeCN−DCM (1:1:3 v/v/v) allowed the separation of siphonodin (3) from lappaceolides (1 and 2). The spectroscopic data obtained for the synthetic sample of lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) were identical to those reported for the isolated materials.1 Furthermore, a single-crystal X-ray diffraction structure of cocrystallized lappaceolides A (1) and B (2) was obtained.8

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for the dimerization step.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Total steps in the longest linear sequence.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly reported failed attempts.

validation.significance: The total synthesis verifies Ragasa's biosynthetic proposal, demonstrating a biomimetic two-step synthesis of lappaceolides A and B from siphonodin via a Cs2CO3-mediated vinylogous-Michael-oxa-Michael domino reaction. It highlights the challenges of nonenzymatic biomimetic syntheses requiring careful kinetic control.

source.title: Biomimetic Total Synthesis of (±)-Lappaceolides A and B.
source.authors: Pallerla RR, Hakola J, Härkönen L, Siitonen JH.
